reasoning: Clement uses the contemporary social reality of the 'good workman' (laborer) receiving 'bread' (wages/sustenance) from an 'employer' as a metaphor for divine reward, confirming the established norm that labor entitles the worker to compensation.
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule uses contemporary labor norms (wages for work) to teach spiritual principles to the community.
rule_date_reasoning: The letter is a contemporary document from the Roman church dated to 96 CE.
materiality_reasoning: The 'bread of his labour' (wages) is a tangible material subsistence resource.
generality_reasoning: The right to earnings is a foundational economic outcome for laborers.
persistence_reasoning: Wages are recurring and episodic based on specific periods of work.
immutability_reasoning: Being a 'workman' is an occupational role one can enter or leave.
contemporarity_reasoning: Clement appeals to a common social practice visible in his own time.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Verbatim treats the payment of workmen as an obvious and attested fact of life.
tautology_reasoning: Being a laborer describes performing work, not necessarily the right to receive pay.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
source_type_description: Religious / scriptural text (NT & apostolic writings)
verbatim: ['For from him come the priests and all the Levites, who serve the altar of God, from him comes the Lord Jesus according to the flesh, from him come the kings and rulers and governors in the succession of Judah, and the other sceptres of his tribes are in no small renown seeing that God promised that thy seed shall be as the stars of heaven.']
reasoning: The text describes the historical and religious rule of the Jewish tradition (which Clement and his audience recognize as a foundational precedent) where specific lineages (Levites/Judah) were granted exclusive access to specific political and religious resources (priesthood/kingship).
rule_polity_reasoning: Clement is referencing the historical and religious precedents of the Levitical and Judaean systems as established in Hebrew scripture.
rule_date_reasoning: The verbatim refers to the established hierarchy of the Second Temple period (post-exilic) as a historical model; -450 represents a central date for these priestly regulations.
materiality_reasoning: Access to priesthood and kingship involve concrete legal/social status with material power.
generality_reasoning: Political and religious rule are foundational societal axes.
persistence_reasoning: Hereditary priesthood and kingship are lifelong and transgenerational positions.
immutability_reasoning: Levitical/Judahite status is strictly fixed by lineage and birth.
contemporarity_reasoning: Clement is recounting the ancestral/historical arrangement of ancient Israel.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes the hereditary distribution of roles as a historical fact.
tautology_reasoning: Lineage does not analytically define holding an office; it is a criterion for it.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
source_type_description: Religious / scriptural text (NT & apostolic writings)
verbatim: ['For to the High Priest his proper ministrations are allotted, and to the priests the proper place has been appointed, and on Levites their proper services have been imposed. The layman is bound by the ordinances for the laity.']
reasoning: Clement describes a structured hierarchy of religious roles where the High Priest, priests, and Levites have exclusive rights to perform specific liturgical functions, contrasting them with the laity.
rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim describes the specific cultic divisions of Israelites (High Priests, Priests, Levites, Laity) as a historical archetype.
rule_date_reasoning: The rule concerns the hereditary and liturgical functions of the Jewish priesthood in the Second Temple era, anchored here to approximately -450.
materiality_reasoning: The right to perform ministrations is a concrete legal/professional right within an institution.
generality_reasoning: Ministrations are recurring religious acts specific to the cultic institution.
persistence_reasoning: High priesthood is usually a lifelong or long-term structural role.
immutability_reasoning: High Priest is a structural legal/religious status, often hereditary or lifelong.
contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to the fixed ordinances of the Jewish temple (past or distant) as a model.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Verbatim states the allotment of duties as plain organizational fact.
tautology_reasoning: Performing specifically 'proper ministrations' is not inherent to the word priest.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
source_type_description: Religious / scriptural text (NT & apostolic writings)
verbatim: ['Not in every place, my brethren, are the daily sacrifices offered or the free-will offerings, or the sin-offerings and trespass-offerings, but only in Jerusalem; and there also the offering is not made in every place, but before the shrine, at the altar, and the offering is first inspected by the High Priest and the ministers already mentioned. Those therefore who do anything contrary to that which is agreeable to his will suffer the penalty of death.']
reasoning: Clement defines a strict rule regarding where and how sacrifices are to be conducted, noting that even for priests, offering sacrifices outside the designated place (Jerusalem) or without inspection is a capital offense.
rule_polity_reasoning: This rule describes the centralized sacrificial laws of Jerusalem and the consequences for unauthorized worship in the Jewish tradition.
rule_date_reasoning: Clement refers to the past cultic laws of Jerusalem, specifically the centralized worship established and maintained in the Second Temple period.
materiality_reasoning: Conducting sacrifices is a professional role; prohibition carries the material penalty of death.
generality_reasoning: Scoped to specific cultic procedures and locations.
persistence_reasoning: Restrictions on priestly conduct are permanent-till-revoked social laws.
immutability_reasoning: Priesthood is a structural religious-birth or legal status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the historical temple cult of Jerusalem.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Verbatim lists the locations and penalties as simple factual reports.
tautology_reasoning: The definition of a priest does not determine where he may offer sacrifice.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
source_type_description: Religious / scriptural text (NT & apostolic writings)
verbatim: ['If sedition and strife and divisions have arisen on my account, I will depart, I will go away whithersoever you will, and I will obey the commands of the people; only let the flock of Christ have peace with the presbyters set over it.']
reasoning: The text describes an established hierarchical order where 'presbyters' are 'set over' the 'flock' (the community). Clement presents this as a normative rule that should not be disrupted by sedition.
rule_polity_reasoning: The hierarchy of presbyters over the laity is a central tenet of the communal order Clement is defending.
rule_date_reasoning: The rule reflects the organizational norms Clement sought to enforce in 96 CE.
materiality_reasoning: Political power over a community represents concrete social and institutional authority.
generality_reasoning: Authority over a 'flock' spans many aspects of communal life.
persistence_reasoning: Authority persists for the duration of the presbyter's appointment.
immutability_reasoning: Presbyter is a social identity acquired through appointment.
contemporarity_reasoning: Clement refers to the contemporary church structure.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Asserts the hierarchy (presbyters 'set over') as the normative social order.
tautology_reasoning: Authority is an attribute granted to the role, not the definition of the role itself.

perseus_title: The First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians
source_type_description: Religious / scriptural text (NT & apostolic writings)
verbatim: ['We know that many among ourselves have given themselves to bondage that they might ransom others. Many have delivered themselves to slavery, and provided food for others with the price they received for themselves.']
reasoning: Clement cites contemporary practices within his own community where individuals relinquish their freedom (selling themselves into slavery) to acquire resources (food or ransom) for other members.
rule_polity_reasoning: Clement describes a practice within the contemporary Christian community of voluntary servitude for the benefit of others.
rule_date_reasoning: Clement cites this as a practice 'among ourselves' during the time of his writing in 96 CE.
materiality_reasoning: Freedom from bondage is a foundational material resource regarding bodily autonomy.
generality_reasoning: Slavery vs. freedom is a major structural axis of ancient society.
persistence_reasoning: Selling oneself into slavery is a permanent, lifelong status change.
immutability_reasoning: Christians are a religious community; slavery/freedom is a legal birth or structural status.
contemporarity_reasoning: Clement explicitly mentions 'among ourselves' in the present.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports specific cases of voluntary enslavement as factual occurrences.
tautology_reasoning: Being a Christian does not imply being a slave or relinquishing freedom.

perseus_title: The Epistle to the Philippians of Saint Polycarp
source_type_description: Religious / scriptural text (NT & apostolic writings)
verbatim: ['For eyeryone who does not confess that Jesus Christ lias come in the flesh is an anti-Christ; and whosoever does not confess the testimony of the Cross is of the devil: and whosoever perverts the oracles of the Lord for his own lusts, and says that there is neither resurrection nor judgment,—this man is the first-born of Satan.']
reasoning: The text sets a boundary based on religious confession; those who deviate from the core dogma are labeled as 'anti-Christ' or 'of the devil', which in the context of this letter serves as a rule for their social and ritual exclusion.
resource: Right to be part of the community
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule defines the boundaries of communal belonging and identifies those to be excluded based on doctrinal deviation.
rule_date_reasoning: Anchored to the date of Polycarp's letter to the Philippians (c. 110 CE) where he defines these criteria for exclusion.
materiality_reasoning: Exclusion from the community (excommunication) results in loss of social support and legal standing.
generality_reasoning: Communal inclusion/exclusion represents a foundational societal axis for the early Christians.
persistence_reasoning: Exclusion persists as long as the theological deviation (belief) remains unrepented.
immutability_reasoning: Heretic status is defined by adherence to a specific belief, which is durable but changeable.
contemporarity_reasoning: Polycarp is identifying current individuals and groups within his own society for exclusion.
opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The author identifies these people as 'first-born of Satan,' a factual label for social exclusion in this group.
tautology_reasoning: Holding specific dogmatic views is not definitionally the same as being 'socially excluded'.
